findings.description: Email is interpolated directly into the SQL string via an f-string, enabling SQL injection and authentication bypass.
Expiry comparison is inverted (expires_at > now), so still-valid sessions are deleted/rejected while already-expired sessions are accepted, causing an auth bypass.

Lockout check precedes password verification with no time-based reset, so once failed_attempts hits the cap the account is permanently locked even with a correct password (LOCKOUT_MINUTES never applied).
find_user_by_email runs SELECT * FROM users and linearly scans every row in Python instead of a parameterized WHERE email = ? query, an O(n) memory/time hit that degrades with table size.
Unknown emails return before the expensive PBKDF2 hash while valid emails compute it, creating a timing oracle for user enumeration.
PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 uses only 100,000 iterations, below current OWASP guidance (~600,000) for this algorithm.
Sessions are held in an in-memory dict, so they are lost on restart and not shared across multiple worker processes or instances.
The shared _sessions dict is mutated from multiple methods without locking, risking races under concurrent (multi-threaded) server use.
rotate_token's sliding-renewal contract is broken by the inverted validate_session check, so it will refuse to rotate live tokens.
Email is matched case-sensitively (both here and in the login SQL), which may reject valid logins when stored casing differs from input.
datetime.utcnow() is deprecated as of Python 3.12 and yields naive datetimes; recurs at lines 105 and 147.
Constant LOCKOUT_MINUTES is declared but never used, leaving the intended time-based lockout expiry unimplemented.
The 'time' module is imported but never used.
